---
title: "22 · Data, Models, Empirical Risk Minimisation"
subtitle: "Every ML algorithm is a way to pick the best member of a function family — by minimising the average loss on the training set."
lesson-id: "mml.models-and-erm"
tier: "mml"
prerequisites: ["mml.constrained-convex-optimisation"]
unlocks: ["mml.graphical-models-model-selection"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you compute gradients of an MSE loss? — see
   [MML — 15, Vector and Matrix Gradients](../15-vector-matrix-gradients/index.qmd).
2. Do you know what i.i.d. samples are? — see
   [MML — 17, Probability Spaces and Bayes](../17-probability-spaces-bayes/index.qmd).
3. Can you state MLE? — see [MML — 19](../19-conjugacy-change-of-variables/index.qmd).
4. Can you state gradient descent's update rule? — [MML — 20](../20-gradient-descent/index.qmd).

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

A regression dataset and three candidate models: a constant, a line, and a 5-degree polynomial. The widget shows training data and the model's fit. Increase model complexity to overfit; reduce to underfit.

```{ojs}
viewof N = Inputs.range([5, 60], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "N data points:"})
viewof complexity = Inputs.range([0, 9], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "polynomial degree:"})
viewof noise = Inputs.range([0, 1], {value: 0.3, step: 0.05, label: "noise std:"})
viewof seed = Inputs.range([1, 50], {value: 7, step: 1, label: "seed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function rng(s) { return () => { s = (s * 1103515245 + 12345) & 0x7fffffff; return s / 0x7fffffff; }; }
  const r = rng(seed);
  const xs = [], ys = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    const x = -1 + 2 * (i + 0.5) / N + (r() - 0.5) * 0.1;
    const truth = Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * x);
    const y = truth + noise * 2 * (r() - 0.5);
    xs.push(x); ys.push(y);
  }
  // Polynomial fit by normal equations
  const D = complexity + 1;
  // X matrix
  const X = xs.map(x => Array.from({length: D}, (_, k) => Math.pow(x, k)));
  // X^T X
  const XtX = Array.from({length: D}, () => Array(D).fill(0));
  for (let i = 0; i < D; i++) for (let j = 0; j < D; j++) for (let n = 0; n < N; n++) XtX[i][j] += X[n][i] * X[n][j];
  const Xty = Array(D).fill(0);
  for (let i = 0; i < D; i++) for (let n = 0; n < N; n++) Xty[i] += X[n][i] * ys[n];
  // Solve via Gauss-Jordan
  const A = XtX.map((row, i) => row.concat([Xty[i]]));
  for (let i = 0; i < D; i++) {
    let pivot = A[i][i];
    if (Math.abs(pivot) < 1e-12) pivot = 1e-12;
    for (let j = 0; j <= D; j++) A[i][j] /= pivot;
    for (let k = 0; k < D; k++) if (k !== i) {
      const f = A[k][i];
      for (let j = 0; j <= D; j++) A[k][j] -= f * A[i][j];
    }
  }
  const w = A.map(row => row[D]);
  // Predict on a fine grid
  const grid = [], truthGrid = [];
  for (let xx = -1; xx <= 1; xx += 0.01) {
    let yhat = 0;
    for (let k = 0; k < D; k++) yhat += w[k] * Math.pow(xx, k);
    grid.push({x: xx, y: yhat});
    truthGrid.push({x: xx, y: Math.sin(2*Math.PI*xx)});
  }
  // Empirical MSE
  let mse = 0;
  for (let n = 0; n < N; n++) {
    let yhat = 0;
    for (let k = 0; k < D; k++) yhat += w[k] * Math.pow(xs[n], k);
    mse += (yhat - ys[n]) ** 2;
  }
  mse /= N;
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return html`<div>
    <div style="font:13px sans-serif; margin-bottom:4px;">
      Empirical MSE = ${mse.toFixed(4)}.  Model: polynomial degree ${complexity}.
    </div>
    ${Plot.plot({
      width: 460, height: 300, x: {domain: [-1, 1]}, y: {domain: [-2, 2]},
      marks: [
        Plot.line(truthGrid, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#aaa", strokeDasharray: "3 2"}),
        Plot.dot(xs.map((x, i) => ({x, y: ys[i]})), {x: "x", y: "y", r: 3, fill: "steelblue"}),
        Plot.line(grid, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "tomato", strokeWidth: 2})
      ]
    })}
  </div>`;
}
```

Degree 0: underfits (just a constant). Degree 3: matches the truth well. Degree 9 with small $N$: overfits — passes through every data point but wiggles wildly. **Choosing model complexity matters; the training MSE alone doesn't capture this.**

## Wonder

1. As we crank up complexity, the *training* MSE keeps falling. But generalisation to new data eventually gets worse. Why?
2. The "true" function $\sin(2\pi x)$ is degree-infinity (in a Taylor sense). Yet a polynomial of degree 3 generalises better than degree 9 on 20 noisy samples. What goes wrong?
3. ERM (empirical risk minimisation) minimises average loss on the training set. The "true risk" is the expected loss on the population distribution. What's the relationship?
4. We need to *pick* the right model complexity (regularisation, hyperparameters). What's the systematic way?

## Discover

Five problems building ERM, generalisation, regularisation.

**D1.** Define the **empirical risk** $\hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N L(y_n, f_{\boldsymbol{w}}(\boldsymbol{x}_n))$ and the **true risk** $R(\boldsymbol{w}) = \mathbb{E}_{(\boldsymbol{x}, y) \sim P}[L(y, f_{\boldsymbol{w}}(\boldsymbol{x}))]$. State why $\hat R_N \to R$ as $N \to \infty$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Law of large numbers.** For i.i.d. samples from $P$, the empirical average converges to the expectation. With finite $N$ there's *generalisation gap* $|\hat R_N - R|$, which shrinks as $\sqrt{1/N}$ (CLT) — and is what statistical learning theory bounds.
</details>

**D2.** **Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM)** sets $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}} = \mathrm{argmin}_{\boldsymbol{w}} \hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w})$. Show that on a fixed dataset, ERM gives the same answer as MLE under appropriate loss-likelihood correspondence.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
MLE: $\hat \theta = \mathrm{argmax} \log p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \mathrm{argmax} \sum \log p(y_n \mid \boldsymbol{x}_n; \theta)$. ERM with loss $L = -\log p$: $\hat \theta = \mathrm{argmin} \sum -\log p$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**MLE = ERM with negative-log-likelihood loss.** Squared-error loss corresponds to Gaussian likelihood (Lesson 24); cross-entropy corresponds to categorical likelihood; etc. **Every loss function is, secretly, a probabilistic model.**
</details>

**D3.** A model with too few parameters can't represent the truth — **underfitting**. A model with too many overfits the noise — **overfitting**. Argue why the *test* error is a U-shape in model complexity.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Bias-variance decomposition.** True risk = (bias)² + variance + irreducible noise.
- **Bias**: how far the *best* model in the family is from the truth. Decreases as family grows.
- **Variance**: how much the chosen model varies with different training sets. Increases as family grows.
Sum has a minimum at a model class neither too simple nor too complex. Modern phenomenon: very large overparameterised neural networks *escape* this U-curve through implicit regularisation. **Double descent** (Belkin et al. 2019) — re-look at this when reading about it.
</details>

**D4.** **Regularisation** modifies the objective: $\min \hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w}) + \lambda \Omega(\boldsymbol{w})$, with $\Omega$ a penalty. Why does this often help generalisation, even though it makes the training fit *worse*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Regularisation reduces variance by constraining the chosen model — at the cost of slightly higher bias. The trade-off is favourable when variance dominates (small $N$ relative to model complexity). **Ridge** ($\Omega = \lVert \boldsymbol{w}\rVert^2$), **Lasso** ($\Omega = \lVert \boldsymbol{w}\rVert_1$), **early stopping** (implicit regularisation), and **dropout** (stochastic regularisation) all live here.
</details>

**D5.** **Cross-validation** splits the training set into $K$ folds. Hold each out, train on the rest, evaluate on the held-out. Argue why this estimates *generalisation* better than training error alone.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each fold's validation set is *unseen by training* — so its loss is a sample of true generalisation. Averaging over $K$ folds reduces variance. The model selected by minimum cross-validated error is the one that *generalises best to unseen data*, not the one that fits the training set best.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Vladimir Vapnik** and **Alexey Chervonenkis** introduced ERM and what we now call **VC theory** in their 1971 paper *On the uniform convergence of relative frequencies of events to their probabilities*. The paper sat largely unread in the West until **Vapnik** emigrated to the US in 1990 and joined AT&T Bell Labs, where he developed **Support Vector Machines** (1992–95) — directly applying his theoretical framework. ERM remained the rigorous foundation of statistical learning until **Boltzmann-machine** and **deep learning** approaches showed that overparameterised models could generalise *even when* VC dimension was infinite — kicking off two decades of new theory.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**ERM is the universal recipe of supervised ML.** Pick a function family, pick a loss, minimise average loss over training data. Generalisation is *not* guaranteed — it's a tradeoff between bias (family too narrow) and variance (family too wide). Cross-validation is how you pick the right tradeoff; regularisation is how you bias the optimisation toward generalisable solutions.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}
A **supervised learning problem** has data $\mathcal{D} = \{(\boldsymbol{x}_n, y_n)\}_{n=1}^N$ drawn i.i.d. from a population $P(\boldsymbol{x}, y)$. A **model** is a parametric family $\mathcal{F} = \{f_{\boldsymbol{w}} : \boldsymbol{w} \in \Theta\}$. A **loss** $L(y, \hat y)$ measures the cost of predicting $\hat y$ when the truth is $y$.

**Empirical risk:** $\hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N L(y_n, f_{\boldsymbol{w}}(\boldsymbol{x}_n))$.

**True risk:** $R(\boldsymbol{w}) = \mathbb{E}_{P}[L(y, f_{\boldsymbol{w}}(\boldsymbol{x}))]$.

**ERM:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{\mathrm{ERM}} = \mathrm{argmin}_{\boldsymbol{w}} \hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w})$. As $N \to \infty$, $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{\mathrm{ERM}} \to \boldsymbol{w}^* = \mathrm{argmin} R$ under mild conditions (uniform LLN, learnability).

**Bias-variance decomposition.** For squared-error loss:
$$
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat f(\boldsymbol{x}) - y)^2] = \underbrace{(\bar f(\boldsymbol{x}) - \mathbb{E}[y \mid \boldsymbol{x}])^2}_{\text{bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat f(\boldsymbol{x}) - \bar f(\boldsymbol{x}))^2]}_{\text{variance}} + \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}(y \mid \boldsymbol{x})}_{\text{noise}}.
$$

**Regularised ERM:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}} = \mathrm{argmin} \hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w}) + \lambda \Omega(\boldsymbol{w})$. Equivalent (by Lagrange duality) to a constraint on $\Omega$.

**MAP estimation** = regularised ERM with $\Omega = -\log p_{\mathrm{prior}}(\boldsymbol{w})$. **Maximum likelihood** = ERM with $L = -\log p(y \mid \boldsymbol{x})$.

**$K$-fold cross-validation.** Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $K$ folds. Train on $K - 1$, evaluate on the held-out. Average over folds. The model with minimum CV error is selected.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Sūtra.** *ERM = pick function, pick loss, average over data. Generalisation needs bias-variance trade-off (= regularisation = cross-validation).*
:::
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
**Watch out:**

1. *"Lower training loss is always better."* — Only up to capacity. After that, training loss continues to drop while test loss rises (overfitting).
2. *"Cross-validation gives an unbiased estimate of test error."* — Approximately. With $K = N$ (leave-one-out CV), nearly unbiased. With smaller $K$, slightly biased but lower variance.
3. *"MLE is the best estimator."* — Best in the asymptotic regime, but with small $N$ a regularised (MAP) estimator often generalises better.
:::

## Apply

### Worked example

A regression problem with $N = 100$ samples, candidate models of polynomial degrees $1, 3, 9$. Pick the best via 5-fold cross-validation.

**Step 1.** Split data into 5 equal folds of 20 each.

**Step 2.** For each model class (degree 1, 3, 9):
For each fold $k = 1, \dots, 5$: train on the other 4 folds; compute MSE on fold $k$.
Average the 5 MSEs.

**Step 3.** Hypothetically: degree 1 has high bias (averages MSE $\approx 0.5$). Degree 3 has both low (averages $\approx 0.15$). Degree 9 has high variance (averages $\approx 0.4$). **Degree 3 wins.**

**Step 4.** Train the final degree-3 model on all 100 data points using the chosen complexity.

**Conclusion:** Cross-validation found the "sweet spot" — degree 3 has the bias-variance trade-off right. Degree 9 fits the training noise; degree 1 misses the underlying signal.

### Warm

**W1.** Two losses with the same minimum: which is the *one* to use?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Doesn't matter for the optimum, but the *optimisation dynamics* may differ. MSE has nice gradients; Huber loss is more robust to outliers; the choice depends on the application.
</details>

**W2.** Cross-validation error vs. training error: which is usually lower?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Training error is lower — by construction, the model fits the training data. Cross-validation error generalises and is usually higher.
</details>

**W3.** Doubling training data: bias up or down? Variance up or down?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Bias unchanged (model family is fixed). Variance down ($\sqrt{N}$ shrinkage). Generalisation improves.
</details>

### Working

**Wo1.** Show that MSE-ERM on linear models gives the normal equations $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{w} = \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\hat R_N(\boldsymbol{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N} \lVert \boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w} - \boldsymbol{y}\rVert^2$. Setting $\nabla \hat R_N = \tfrac{2}{N} \boldsymbol{X}^\top (\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w} - \boldsymbol{y}) = \boldsymbol{0}$ gives the normal equations. This is the closed-form linear-regression solution (Lesson 24).
</details>

**Wo2.** Cross-entropy loss for classification: $L(y, \hat{\boldsymbol{p}}) = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat p_c$ for one-hot $\boldsymbol{y}$. Show this is the negative log-likelihood of a categorical distribution.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\log P(y \mid \hat{\boldsymbol{p}}) = \log \hat p_{c^*}$ where $c^*$ is the true class. With one-hot $\boldsymbol{y}$: $\log P = \sum_c y_c \log \hat p_c$. Negate to get the loss. **Categorical NLL = cross-entropy.** Confirms D2's equivalence: every loss is a probabilistic model.
</details>

**Wo3.** A model that achieves zero training error but high test error has *memorised*. State two ways to detect memorisation.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(1) **Generalisation gap**: train error close to 0, validation error high → memorisation. (2) **Label-shuffling test** (Zhang et al. 2017): retrain with shuffled labels; if the model still achieves low loss, it's memorising structure that isn't in the labels. **Memorisation is the dominant failure mode of overparametrised models** — and regularisation, data augmentation, and dropout are the main defences.
</details>

### Stretch

**S1.** **PAC learning** says: a hypothesis class $\mathcal{F}$ is *probably approximately correct* learnable if for every $\epsilon, \delta > 0$, there exists $N$ such that with prob ≥ $1 - \delta$, the ERM hypothesis has true risk within $\epsilon$ of the best. Sketch why this is a stronger statement than ERM consistency.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
ERM consistency (asymptotic): $\hat R \to R^*$ as $N \to \infty$. PAC (finite-sample): bound the gap $|R(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}) - R^*|$ uniformly across $\mathcal{F}$, with explicit dependence on $N$, model complexity, and confidence. **VC dimension** characterises which $\mathcal{F}$ are PAC-learnable in $N$ polynomial in the inverse of $\epsilon, \delta$.
</details>

**S2.** **Double descent** (Belkin et al. 2019): for some overparametrised models, generalisation error decreases past the *second* time the model interpolates the training set. Speculate why.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Below interpolation threshold ($N > D$): standard U-curve. At threshold ($N = D$): variance peaks because the model can interpolate only via large weights. Above ($D > N$): many models interpolate; gradient descent + L2 implicit regularisation picks the *minimum-norm* interpolator, which has good test performance. Empirically: deep nets ride above the interpolation threshold and benefit from implicit regularisation. This is *the* central question of modern deep-learning theory.
</details>

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **ERM minimises average loss on training data. Generalisation requires the bias-variance trade-off — under-complex models underfit, over-complex models overfit.**
2. **Loss functions correspond to probabilistic likelihoods; MLE = ERM with NLL; MAP = regularised ERM.**
3. **Cross-validation is the canonical way to choose model complexity (and other hyperparameters) by estimating true risk from data.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Can you describe the bias-variance decomposition in your own words?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without
   peeking at hints?
3. When you read "regularised loss" in a paper, do you instantly see "constrained ERM"?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 23.
- **2 yes:** redo D3 and D5, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* problems D3 and D4.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`mml.constrained-convex-optimisation`](../21-constrained-convex-optimisation/index.qmd) —
  regularised ERM = constrained ERM, by Lagrangian duality.

### What this unlocks

- [`mml.graphical-models-model-selection`](../23-graphical-models-model-selection/index.qmd) — probabilistic modelling layered on top.
- All later lessons (LR, PCA, GMM, SVM) are concrete instances of ERM.

### Sister synthesis lesson

[`t7.statistical-learning-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/01-statistical-learning-theory/index.qmd) — the rigorous PAC / VC-dimension treatment. This lesson provides the working ML view that connects to the Tier 7 theory.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every supervised learning algorithm is ERM under some loss. Every regularisation scheme is a prior or a constraint. Cross-validation is the universal hyperparameter-tuning method.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Generalisation theory (PAC, VC, Rademacher complexity, PAC-Bayes) all build on ERM. Recent learning theory: implicit regularisation, neural tangent kernels, double descent.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Query-cost models are statistical models fit by ERM. Learned indices (Kraska et al. 2018) cast index lookup as regression over keys.
:::
